text: As a brand, Star Wars isn't just on life support at this point. It's already been declared legally dead. The paperwork's been filed. Its friends and relatives have gathered around to mourn it. And it's currently being lowered down 6 ft into the cold, dark embrace of eternal sleep. The only thing that could possibly turn these grim proceedings around is a glorious return to the silver screen in the grandest fashion imaginable. A new movie that would capture the hearts and minds of

audiences worldwide. the kind of cultural lightning bolt that made A New Hope into an instant classic. The point is, Disney needed to do something seriously big to win back the cultural momentum and the fan goodwill they've burned up over the past decades. Not just a big budget movie with a massive marketing push designed to grab headlines, but a statement of intent, saying one thing loud and clear. We are back, [ __ ] and we ain't going anywhere. Or they could just shart out a

completely uninspired and predictable big screen adaptation of the one and only vaguely marketable character that Disney managed to fall ass backwards into over the past decade and spent three entire seasons milking so hard that its nipples are going to need medical grade anti-chafing cream for the next several months. The first theatrical movie about the Mandalorian and Grou, helpfully titled The Mandalorian and Grou. Oh my goodness. Try to contain that wild

creativity, Lucas film, you're making the rest of us look bad, is on the way. And the first trailer dropped a few days ago to thunderous disinterest. Seriously, after 6 years without producing a single movie, this is what you choose as your big comeback. Not a great plan. >> Not only does the trailer itself look boring as [ __ ] showcasing a bunch of uninspired locations and goofy action scenes that look no better than an episode of the TV show while telling us

absolutely nothing about the story and characters. Wow. Can't wait for another 2 hours of that [ __ ] green animatronic puppet wandering around doing the same shtick he's been doing since 2019. They really are stuck in a catch 22 situation with this stupid [ __ ] thing. keeping him as a wordless baby forever would nuke any possibility of progress or development because he literally can't communicate. But on the other hand, the moment he actually starts talking and growing up, he stops

being the cute little baby Yoda that sold more merch than every other Disney Star Wars character combined. That's why he was supposed to leave the show after season 2 because John Favro had literally run out of things to do with him. From a visual point of view, that trailer is so completely soulless and directionless that it reminds me of those AI generated trailers still doing the rounds. Like, what if Lord of the Rings was reimagined as a 1980s action flick? [ __ ] man. I'd watch that. Or

Resident Evil was remade as a 1950s Panovision horror movie. It's nothing but concept art with a bit of animation to it, and it's not even especially creative or interesting. The casting isn't exactly blowing my underwear apart, either. You know, there was a time when seeing Sigourney Weaver in a major franchise movie was kind of a big deal. Like a stamp of approval from a respected actress who could afford to be selective about her roles. I mean, if Ellen [ __ ] Ripley decides to show up

in your movie, then you're probably in for some kind of good time at least. But after looking at some of the absolute slop she's done in recent years for a quick and easy paycheck, well, maybe not so much. She's become like a female Samuel L. Jackson who used to be the epitome of cool. The kind of actor that could elevate any movie he was in by his sheer presence alone, then lost the ability to say no about five years ago, and just shows up in basically any

production that'll have him. Now, [ __ ] man. Last year alone, he was in like eight different projects. >> I don't remember asking you a godamn thing. >> Speaking of actors who can't say no to a role, it's nice to see Pedro Pascal back in action for the first time in literally weeks. I mean, I say he's in the movie, but for all I know, he recorded his lines from a [ __ ] sound booth in his pajamas and let some stunt guy do the actual performance on set. Wouldn't be the first time either. He's

made no secret of the fact that he'd outgrown The Mandalorian after season 2 and had no interest in walking around with a [ __ ] helmet on his head 95% of the time. So, unless it gets conveniently destroyed in the first 5 minutes and he's allowed to have that glorious mustache on display, I'm not exactly expecting great things from him with this one. But the bigger problem with all this is that well, nobody really gives a [ __ ] about The Mandalorian anymore. Yeah, it was kind

of a big deal on Disney Plus five years ago for maybe two seasons, but marginal success on an obscure streaming platform doesn't translate into widespread appeal and billion dollar box office halls. Two things which Star Wars movies really need in order to succeed these days. It feels more like a movie made through a process of elimination instead of any kind of artistic drive. Like a bunch of moneymen sat down in a boardroom, analyzed user trends and merchandise sales, and concluded that The

Mandalorian was the property most likely to generate a profit. Just another dry clinical business decision from a company that stopped being about artistic merit like 15 years ago. And that's even assuming everyone who saw it still has a favorable opinion of The Mandalorian, which let's be honest, hasn't been true for a long time now. Season 3 was a tedious, laughable disaster that proved that Grou had well and truly outsted upon us the horrors of Jack Black and Lizo trying to act

and pushed Mando to the periphery of his own show in favor of his more diverse and politically acceptable replacement, Bokeatan. yet another generic Star Wars girl boss. So famously lacking in anything even resembling a personality that she literally triggered an existential crisis in the actress playing and destroyed her confidence in her own acting ability for years afterwards. Damn, man. Imagine knowing you'd written a character so thoroughly [ __ ] that it made the actress want to

give up acting. Oh man. Oh god. Oh man. What I'm trying to say with all this pesh is that even though The Mandalorian was probably the best of the utter sludge being pumped onto the Star Wars section on Disney Plus, with one obvious exception, even at its peak, it never really crossed into mainstream awareness. The average person on the street had no [ __ ] idea who Gingarin, Bokeatan, or Grou were. And let's be real, its peak was a long time ago at this point. Now, it's seen

as kind of an unfunny joke with no punchline. And the disaster of season 3 has probably caused a lot of people to reassess their feelings on the previous two. Look past the cutesy puppets, the shameless nostalgia bait, and the halfdecent action scenes, and you begin to realize that it was still shallow, mindless slop, it was just packaged slightly better. So, is there any way for Star Wars to come back to life at this point? Well, I'm no expert, but one thing I can tell you is that if it's

been more than half a decade since your last movie, and that movie was Rise of Skywalker, then you really need to come back with the mother of all bangs if you expect to win people over again. And not just one bang either, preferably several in a row to prove it wasn't a fluke and that there's still plenty of life left in your franchise. Somehow, I don't think The Mandalorian is going to accomplish any of those things, but hey, it's probably going to be amusing watching them try.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today. Go away now.
